title: How to Read John
subtitle: The theological Gospel — "that you may believe that Jesus is the Messiah, the Son of God."

authorship.author: John the son of Zebedee — fisherman, apostle, and “the disciple whom Jesus loved” (John 13:23; 19:26; 20:2; 21:7, 20). The fourth Gospel never names its author directly, but the evidence converges on John: (1) The “beloved disciple” is an eyewitness (19:35; 21:24) present at the Last Supper, the cross, and the empty tomb. (2) Early and unanimous tradition: Irenaeus (c. AD 180), Clement of Alexandria, Origen, and Eusebius all identify the author as John the apostle writing from Ephesus in old age. (3) The Gospel’s intimate knowledge of Jerusalem topography (Pool of Bethesda, Pool of Siloam, the Pavement/Gabbatha) is confirmed by archaeology. (4) The eyewitness claim of 19:35 and the closing verse of 21:24 point to direct authorial witness. Liberal scholarship has proposed an anonymous Johannine community or the Elder John of Ephesus; the traditional identification with the apostle remains the best-attested position.
authorship.date: c. AD 85–95, probably from Ephesus, making it the latest of the four Gospels. The Rylands Papyrus (P52, c. AD 125) — the earliest surviving NT manuscript fragment — contains John 18:31-33, 37-38, confirming the Gospel was in circulation in Egypt by the early second century. A pre-AD 70 date has been argued from the Temple language in chs 2, 5, and 10, which reads as description of a standing building; the majority of scholars favour a post-70 date for the final composition. The three Johannine letters and Revelation share vocabulary and theological concerns with the Gospel, suggesting a common Ephesian milieu.
authorship.prompt: John states his purpose explicitly — a rarity in the Gospels: “These are written that you may believe that Jesus is the Messiah, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name” (20:31). The Gospel was written to produce and sustain faith in the person of Jesus as divine Son. It supplements the Synoptics rather than duplicates them: John omits the Baptism, the Transfiguration, the Olivet Discourse, and the institution of the Lord’s Supper as a formal narrative, while providing material found nowhere else — the wedding at Cana, Nicodemus, the Samaritan woman, the raising of Lazarus, the Farewell Discourse (chs 14–17), and the resurrection appearances to Mary Magdalene and Thomas. Its elevated Christology (“In the beginning was the Word”) addresses late first-century challenges to the full divinity of Christ and lays the foundation for all subsequent trinitarian theology.

sections.heading: What Kind of Book Is This?
sections.content:
John is unlike the other three Gospels. There are no parables, no exorcisms, no Sermon on the Mount. Instead: seven carefully chosen "signs" (miracles), seven "I am" sayings, and long theological discourses. John writes at a higher altitude — his Jesus speaks in cosmic terms about light and darkness, truth and lies, life and death.

The prologue (1:1-18) sets the key: Jesus is the eternal Logos (Word) who was with God and was God, now become flesh. Everything that follows is commentary on that claim. The book builds to Thomas's confession — "My Lord and my God!" — which is where John wants every reader to arrive.

sections.heading: Historical Context
sections.content: John is the last gospel written, probably between AD 85 and 95, traditionally attributed to the apostle John ("the disciple whom Jesus loved"). It is deliberately different from the Synoptics: no parables, no exorcisms, no birth narrative, no Last Supper institution. Instead, seven signs, seven "I am" statements, and extended theological discourses. The discovery of the Pool of Bethesda (5:2) and the Pool of Siloam (9:7) with precisely the architectural features John describes has confirmed the gospel's detailed knowledge of pre-AD 70 Jerusalem.

title: How to Read Acts

authorship.author: Luke the physician (Col 4:14), a Gentile convert and travelling companion of Paul. Acts is the second volume of a two-part work addressed to Theophilus (Luke 1:3; Acts 1:1). The “we” sections (16:10–17; 20:5–21:18; 27:1–28:16) place Luke as an eyewitness participant in significant portions of the Pauline mission. Irenaeus, Clement of Alexandria, and the Muratorian Canon (c. AD 170) unanimously identify Luke as author.
authorship.date: c. AD 62–64, most likely during Paul's Roman imprisonment (28:30–31). The abrupt ending — Paul awaiting trial — is most naturally explained if Luke wrote before the outcome was known. The silence about Jerusalem’s destruction (AD 70) and Paul’s death (c. AD 67) supports an early 60s date.
authorship.prompt: Luke traces the Gospel from Jerusalem to Rome, fulfilling the commission of 1:8 (“you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth”). Acts defends the legitimacy of the Gentile mission, demonstrates the continuity of the church with Israel, and shows that the gospel’s advance is providentially directed through opposition, imprisonment, and persecution. It is the indispensable bridge between the Gospels and the Epistles.

subtitle: The sequel to Luke's Gospel — the Spirit empowers the church to carry Jesus' mission to the ends of the earth.

sections.heading: What Kind of Book Is This?
sections.content:
Acts is a continuation of Luke's Gospel. Luke told the story of what Jesus "began to do and teach"; Acts tells what he continued to do through his Spirit-empowered followers. It is part history, part theology, part adventure story — shipwrecks, prison breaks, riots, and speeches before kings.

The geographical structure tells the theological story: Jerusalem (the Jewish centre) → Samaria (the half-outsiders) → the ends of the earth (Rome, the Gentile capital). The Holy Spirit drives every expansion, often over the objections of the human characters.

sections.heading: Historical Context
sections.content: Acts is the second volume of Luke's two-part work, covering roughly 30 years from Jesus' ascension (c.AD 30) to Paul's imprisonment in Rome (c.AD 62). The narrative traces the gospel's geographic expansion: Jerusalem → Judea → Samaria → the ends of the earth (1:8). Paul's missionary journeys are set against a well-documented Roman administrative backdrop — proconsuls, politarchs, and Asiarchs are all confirmed by inscriptions. The book ends abruptly with Paul in Rome, possibly because Luke wrote while events were still unfolding.

title: How to Read Ezra
subtitle: Return from exile, rebuilding the temple, and the painful cost of covenant renewal.

authorship.author: Traditionally attributed to Ezra the scribe-priest, possibly compiled from his memoirs (chs.7-10, written in first person) and official Persian documents (chs.1-6, including Aramaic imperial correspondence). Ezra was a descendant of Aaron through Zadok, trained in the law of Moses, and held the title 'scribe skilled in the Law of Moses' — a new kind of religious leader for a post-temple world.
authorship.date: Events span 538-458 BC (the return under Zerubbabel through Ezra's reforms). Final compilation likely 5th-4th century BC.
authorship.prompt: To record the fulfilment of Jeremiah's prophecy: after 70 years, God brought his people home. But return is not restoration — the book wrestles with how to rebuild identity when the temple is smaller, the walls are broken, and the community has intermarried with the surrounding nations.

sections.heading: What Kind of Book Is This?
sections.content: Ezra is a theological history of return and renewal. It is bilingual (Hebrew and Aramaic) and incorporates official Persian documents — edicts, letters, and administrative records — giving it a documentary quality unique in the OT. The narrative moves in two waves: the first return under Zerubbabel (chs.1-6) and the second under Ezra (chs.7-10), separated by roughly 60 years of silence.

outline.label: First Return & Temple Rebuilt
outline.note: Cyrus's decree, altar restored, temple completed (516 BC)

outline.label: Ezra's Return & Reform
outline.note: Ezra arrives, discovers intermarriage, leads painful covenant renewal

sections.heading: Key Themes to Watch For
themes: The Word of God as Authority

sections.heading: Historical Context
sections.content: Ezra spans the Persian period (539-458 BC). Cyrus the Great's policy of returning captive peoples to their homelands is confirmed by the Cyrus Cylinder. The rebuilt temple (completed 516 BC) was modest compared to Solomon's — old men who remembered the first temple wept (3:12). The intermarriage crisis (chs.9-10) reflects the genuine danger of cultural absorption: a tiny community surrounded by larger populations risked losing its distinctive identity. Ezra's solution was painful but preserved the community that would eventually produce the Scriptures, the synagogue, and the matrix of early Christianity.

plan.label: Cyrus's decree — the exile ends
The altar rebuilt — worship before walls

images.caption: Ezra and the restoration of worship
images.credit: Julius Schnorr von Carolsfeld · Public domain

purpose: To record the return of Jewish exiles from Babylon and the rebuilding of the temple under Zerubbabel and the spiritual reformation under Ezra. The book demonstrates God's faithfulness in fulfilling his promise of restoration and reestablishing covenant identity.

key_verses.text: Any of his people among you may go up to Jerusalem in Judah and build the temple of the Lord, the God of Israel, the God who is in Jerusalem, and may their God be with them.
key_verses.why: Cyrus's decree—God's sovereignty over pagan kings to fulfill prophecy.

key_verses.text: For Ezra had devoted himself to the study and observance of the Law of the Lord, and to teaching its decrees and laws in Israel.
key_verses.why: Ezra's model—study, practice, teach. The pattern for biblical leadership.

key_verses.text: Though we are slaves, our God has not forsaken us in our bondage. He has shown us kindness in the sight of the kings of Persia: He has granted us new life to rebuild the house of our God and repair its ruins, and he has given us a wall of protection in Judah and Jerusalem.
key_verses.why: Ezra's confession—gratitude for God's grace in restoration.

christ_in: The rebuilt temple, though less glorious than Solomon's, points forward to Christ as the greater temple (Hag 2:9; John 2:19). Ezra's role as priest-scribe who brings the people back to the Word anticipates Christ who is the Word made flesh. The purification of the community from foreign entanglement foreshadows the holy people Christ creates.

at_a_glance.author: Ezra (with earlier sources)
at_a_glance.key_theme: God restores his people through his Word and worship

title: How to Read Nehemiah
subtitle: Rebuilding walls, renewing covenant, and the faith it takes to work with a sword in one hand and a trowel in the other.

authorship.author: Nehemiah son of Hakaliah, cupbearer to the Persian king Artaxerxes I. His memoirs (the 'Nehemiah Memoir') form the backbone of the book, written in vivid first-person prose. The book was likely compiled together with Ezra as a single work in the post-exilic period.
authorship.date: Events span 445-432 BC. Nehemiah arrives in Jerusalem in the 20th year of Artaxerxes I (445 BC) and serves as governor for 12 years.
authorship.prompt: To record the rebuilding of Jerusalem's walls and the renewal of covenant life. Nehemiah shows what faithful leadership looks like under opposition: pray first, plan carefully, work tirelessly, and refuse to be distracted.

sections.heading: What Kind of Book Is This?
sections.content: Nehemiah is a leadership memoir — one of the most personal books in the OT. Nehemiah narrates in first person, records his prayers in real-time (the famous "arrow prayers" of 2:4, 4:4, 5:19, 6:14), and documents opposition with the precision of an administrator. The book shows that spiritual renewal and practical work are not opposites but partners.

outline.label: Wall Rebuilt
outline.note: Prayer, planning, construction in 52 days despite opposition

outline.label: Covenant Renewed
outline.note: Ezra reads the Law, the people weep, the covenant is signed

outline.label: Community Organized
outline.note: Repopulation, dedication, and Nehemiah's final reforms

sections.heading: Key Themes to Watch For
themes: The Word of God Read Publicly

sections.heading: Historical Context
sections.content: Nehemiah arrives in a Jerusalem that has been partially resettled but remains vulnerable — the walls are broken, the population is small, and the surrounding governors (Sanballat, Tobiah, Geshem) actively oppose rebuilding. The Elephantine Papyri from Egypt confirm the names and political dynamics of this period. The 52-day wall construction (6:15) is a feat of emergency engineering, not normal building. The great covenant-renewal ceremony of chs.8-10, where Ezra reads the Torah to the assembled people, may be the origin of the synagogue reading cycle.

plan.label: Nehemiah's prayer — the model of intercession
Night inspection of the walls — leadership begins with seeing
Sword and trowel — working under threat
Ezra reads the Law — the people weep, then feast
The great confession prayer

purpose: To record the rebuilding of Jerusalem's walls under Nehemiah's leadership and the spiritual renewal of the community. Nehemiah shows how godly leadership, prayer, and perseverance can accomplish God's purposes despite fierce opposition.

key_verses.text: When I heard these things, I sat down and wept. For some days I mourned and fasted and prayed before the God of heaven.
key_verses.why: Nehemiah's response to crisis—grief, fasting, prayer before action.

key_verses.text: After I looked things over, I stood up and said to the nobles, the officials and the rest of the people, "Don't be afraid of them. Remember the Lord, who is great and awesome, and fight for your families, your sons and your daughters, your wives and your homes."
key_verses.why: Courage grounded in remembering who God is.

key_verses.text: Nehemiah said, "Go and enjoy choice food and sweet drinks, and send some to those who have nothing prepared. This day is holy to our Lord. Do not grieve, for the joy of the Lord is your strength."
key_verses.why: Joy in God's Word—strength for covenant living.

christ_in: Nehemiah as cupbearer who leaves the king's presence to rebuild and restore prefigures Christ who left heaven to rebuild a broken humanity. His leadership by example, prayer, and perseverance reflects Christ's shepherding. The rebuilt wall creating separation for holiness anticipates the church as a holy community set apart in Christ.

at_a_glance.key_theme: Rebuilding requires prayer, courage, and covenant faithfulness

title: How to Read Esther
subtitle: Providence without miracles — the book where God's name never appears but his hand is everywhere.

authorship.author: Anonymous. The author knows the Persian court intimately — protocol, architecture, administrative procedures, even the names of Xerxes' eunuchs (confirmed by Persian records). Possibly Mordecai himself or a court scribe with access to royal records.
authorship.date: Events occur during the reign of Xerxes I (Ahasuerus), 486-465 BC. The story spans approximately 10 years (483-473 BC). Composition likely 5th-4th century BC.
authorship.prompt: To show that God protects his people even when he seems absent. Esther never mentions God by name — the only OT book with this distinction — yet the entire plot turns on 'coincidences' so perfectly timed that no reader can miss the hidden hand.

sections.heading: What Kind of Book Is This?
sections.content: Esther is a court narrative — a story of political intrigue, ethnic threat, and providential reversal set in the Persian empire. It reads like a thriller: banquets, beauty contests, assassination plots, last-minute rescues, and dramatic reversals. The absence of God's name is not absence of God — it is a literary strategy that forces the reader to see providence in the pattern of events rather than in miraculous intervention.

outline.label: Esther Becomes Queen
outline.note: Vashti deposed, Esther chosen, Mordecai saves the king

outline.label: Haman's Plot
outline.note: Genocide decreed, Esther risks her life, the first banquet

outline.label: The Great Reversal
outline.note: Haman falls, Jews triumph, Purim established

sections.heading: Key Themes to Watch For
themes: The Sovereignty of God Without Naming God

sections.heading: Historical Context
sections.content: The story is set in Susa, the winter capital of the Persian Empire, during the reign of Xerxes I (the biblical Ahasuerus). Xerxes' massive banquet (ch.1) fits the historical context of his preparations for the Greek campaign (480 BC). The Persian postal system, the irrevocable nature of royal decrees, and the administrative structure of 127 provinces are all confirmed by Greek historians and Persian records. The festival of Purim, established in ch.9, is still celebrated by Jews worldwide — making Esther one of the most practically influential books in the canon.

images.caption: Esther before King Ahasuerus

purpose: To record God's providential preservation of the Jewish people in Persia through Esther and Mordecai, and to explain the origin of Purim. Though God's name never appears, his sovereign hand guides every 'coincidence' to deliver his people from genocide.

key_verses.text: For if you remain silent at this time, relief and deliverance for the Jews will arise from another place, but you and your father's family will perish. And who knows but that you have come to your royal position for such a time as this?
key_verses.why: Mordecai's challenge—providence, purpose, and the call to act.

key_verses.text: "Go, gather together all the Jews who are in Susa, and fast for me. Do not eat or drink for three days, night or day. I and my attendants will fast likewise. When this is done, I will go to the king, even though it is against the law. And if I perish, I perish."
key_verses.why: Esther's resolve—faith expressed in costly action.

key_verses.text: On the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, the month of Adar, the edict commanded by the king was to be carried out. On this day the enemies of the Jews had hoped to overpower them, but now the tables were turned and the Jews got the upper hand over those who hated them.
key_verses.why: The great reversal—God turning intended destruction into deliverance.

christ_in: Though unnamed, God's providence in Esther mirrors his sovereign protection of the messianic line—the Jews must survive for Christ to come. Esther's intercession, risking her life for her people, foreshadows Christ's greater intercession. The reversal from death sentence to deliverance anticipates the ultimate reversal of crucifixion to resurrection.

title: How to Read Job
subtitle: The oldest question in the world — why do the innocent suffer? — answered not with an explanation but with a Person.

authorship.author: Anonymous. Jewish tradition suggests Moses; others propose Solomon or an unknown sage. The prose framework (chs.1-2, 42:7-17) may be older than the poetic dialogue (chs.3-41). The author demonstrates mastery of poetry, theology, natural history, and legal procedure.
authorship.date: The setting is patriarchal (no mention of Israel, the law, the temple, or the covenant). Composition dates range from the Solomonic era (10th century BC) to the exile (6th century BC). The archaic Hebrew of the poetry suggests an early date for at least the core material.
authorship.prompt: To wrestle with the problem of innocent suffering — not by providing a tidy answer but by leading the reader into the whirlwind where God himself speaks. Job demolishes the simple equation that suffering = punishment and rebuilds faith on a different foundation: encounter with God.

sections.heading: What Kind of Book Is This?
sections.content: Job is a dramatic poem framed by a prose narrative. The prose prologue (chs.1-2) and epilogue (42:7-17) surround 39 chapters of poetry — the longest sustained poetic dialogue in the Bible. The genre is unique: part legal trial, part philosophical debate, part worship. Do not read Job as history alone or philosophy alone — it is theological drama, and the drama matters as much as the theology.

outline.label: Prose Prologue
outline.note: The test — is Job's faith transactional?

outline.label: Dialogue Cycles
outline.note: Three rounds of debate with three friends, increasingly bitter

outline.label: Elihu's Speeches
outline.note: The young man who thinks he has the answer

outline.label: God Speaks
outline.note: The whirlwind — 77 questions, zero explanations

outline.label: Prose Epilogue
outline.note: Repentance, restoration, and vindication

sections.heading: Historical Context
sections.content: Job belongs to the international wisdom tradition — similar suffering-righteous-person texts exist from Mesopotamia (Ludlul bel nemeqi, the "Babylonian Job") and Egypt (the Dispute of a Man with His Ba). But Job goes further than any parallel: the friends' retribution theology is not just challenged but demolished by God himself (42:7). The land of Uz is probably in Edom or northern Arabia. The absence of Israelite religious markers (no temple, no Torah, no covenant) universalises the story: Job's question belongs to humanity, not just Israel.

plan.label: The prologue — the test the reader sees but Job does not
Job's curse — the day he wishes he was never born
"I know that my Redeemer lives" — hope in the ruins
God speaks from the whirlwind — 77 questions

images.caption: Job's suffering — tested by affliction
images.credit: Julius Schnorr von Carolsfeld · Public domain

purpose: To explore the mystery of innocent suffering and vindicate God's justice through the story of a righteous man who loses everything yet clings to faith. Job confronts the inadequacy of simplistic retribution theology and reveals that relationship with God transcends material blessing.

key_verses.text: and said: "Naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked I will depart. The Lord gave and the Lord has taken away; may the name of the Lord be praised."
key_verses.why: Job's initial response—worship in loss, acknowledging God's sovereignty.

key_verses.text: Though he slay me, yet will I hope in him; I will surely defend my ways to his face.
key_verses.why: Job's defiant faith—trusting God even when God seems hostile.

key_verses.text: I know that my redeemer lives, and that in the end he will stand on the earth.
key_verses.why: Job's confession of a living Redeemer—hope beyond death.

key_verses.text: My ears had heard of you but now my eyes have seen you.
key_verses.why: Job's transformation—from theology about God to encounter with God.

christ_in: Job's suffering as a righteous man foreshadows Christ's innocent suffering. His role as intercessor for his friends (42:8-10) anticipates Christ's priestly intercession. The 'Redeemer who lives' (19:25) points to Christ who conquers death. Job's restoration after suffering mirrors Christ's exaltation after the cross.

at_a_glance.key_theme: Trusting God's wisdom when life makes no sense

title: How to Read the Psalms
subtitle: Israel's prayer book and hymnal — 150 poems covering every human emotion from the pit of despair to the peak of praise.

authorship.author: Multiple authors across centuries. David is credited with 73 psalms; Asaph with 12; the Sons of Korah with 11; Solomon with 2; Moses with 1 (Ps 90); Ethan and Heman with 1 each. 50 are anonymous. The Psalter was compiled over 500+ years, with its final five-book structure reflecting deliberate editorial arrangement.
authorship.date: Individual psalms span from the time of Moses (c.1400 BC, Ps 90) to the post-exilic period (Ps 137, after 586 BC). The final compilation was likely completed in the 4th-3rd century BC.
authorship.prompt: To give Israel — and the church — a vocabulary for speaking to God in every circumstance: praise, lament, thanksgiving, confession, rage, trust, wonder, and despair. The Psalms do not describe the spiritual life from a distance; they perform it.

sections.heading: What Kind of Book Is This?
sections.content: The Psalms are not a book to be read straight through but a library to be lived in. They are poems, prayers, and songs — designed to be spoken aloud, sung in worship, and memorised for crisis. The Hebrew title is Tehillim ("praises"), though nearly a third of the psalms are laments. This is significant: Israel's praise-book is full of weeping. Honest grief is a form of worship.

sections.heading: Literary Structure
sections.content: The Psalter is divided into five books, traditionally seen as mirroring the five books of Moses:

outline.label: Book I (Psalms 1-41)
outline.note: Mostly Davidic — personal faith, enemies, trust

outline.label: Book II (Psalms 42-72)
outline.note: Korah psalms, Davidic psalms — national concerns

outline.label: Book III (Psalms 73-89)
outline.note: Asaph and Korah — crisis of faith, covenant questions

outline.label: Book IV (Psalms 90-106)
outline.note: The LORD reigns — response to exile's crisis

outline.label: Book V (Psalms 107-150)
outline.note: Hallelujah collection — pilgrimage and praise

sections.heading: Historical Context
sections.content: The Psalms were Israel's worship songs — used in the temple, at festivals, during pilgrimages, and in daily prayer. The superscriptions ("To the director of music," "A maskil," "A miktam") are performance instructions, not later additions. The Dead Sea Scrolls contain psalms manuscripts that confirm the Psalter was essentially fixed by the 2nd century BC. Jesus quoted the Psalms more than any other OT book, and the early church sang them as their primary hymnal. The Psalms remain the most-read portion of the OT in Christian devotion worldwide.

The cross psalm — Jesus' cry and vindication
David's confession — the model of repentance
The crisis psalm — "until I entered the sanctuary"
